ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Schezwan-style stir-fried chicken. FOODON_03302757 (chicken dish) is the closest parent class available. A more specific term for spicy stir-fried chicken with chilli sauce is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Schezwan-style stir-fried chicken. A00GJ (cooked chicken meat) is used as the nearest defensible parent anchor. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this composite dish preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to Schezwan chicken as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not cover prepared ethnic food dishes at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of Spicy Schezwan Chicken in Singapore is approximately 300 g, typically comprising stir-fried chicken pieces coated in Schezwan sauce, served as a main dish, often accompanied by steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, stir-fried (FDC ID 171477) combined with estimated sauce contribution from USDA SR Legacy condiment data
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Spicy Schezwan Chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central stir-fried chicken data (FDC ID 171477) with estimated contributions from Schezwan sauce ingredients (chilli, soy sauce, oil, garlic, ginger) using USDA SR Legacy component data. Input record values (energy 162.5 kcal, protein 11.25 g, fat 8.75 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was consulted but does not contain a specific Schezwan chicken entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — analogous estimate based on mixed chicken and sauce dish with moderate carbohydrate from sauce ingredients.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Spicy Schezwan Chicken. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for mixed stir-fried chicken dishes with sauce. The carbohydrate content (10 g/100 g) derives primarily from Schezwan sauce (sugar, cornstarch thickener) and vegetables; these are moderate-GI contributors diluted by the dominant protein and fat matrix of the chicken. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (30 g from 300 g portion) / 100 = 13.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g from Schezwan sauce and soy sauce; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium, representing 39–45% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-sauce preparation or a low-sodium soy sauce variant, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium accompaniments such as salted vegetables or processed rice dishes to keep total meal sodium below 800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate is 10 g/100 g (GI ~45, low category); a 300 g serving provides approximately 30 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 13.5, which is moderate and manageable within a controlled meal plan.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes can include this dish in a controlled meal plan by monitoring total carbohydrate intake for the full meal, requesting reduced-sugar Schezwan sauce, and pairing with non-starchy vegetables rather than white rice to limit postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.05 g/100 g; however, sodium at 300 mg/100 g and total fat at 8.75 g/100 g from stir-frying oil require attention in a CVD-managed diet.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal added oil and reduced-sodium soy sauce, and limit portion size to 200 g to keep saturated fat below 4 g and sodium below 600 mg per eating occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bell peppers and chilli in Schezwan sauce are nightshade-family ingredients; solanine alkaloids in nightshades may aggravate joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis who are nightshade-sensitive should request substitution of bell peppers and chilli with non-nightshade vegetables such as broccoli or bok choy, while retaining ginger and garlic for their anti-inflammatory gingerol and allicin content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Schezwan Chicken is a popular stir-fried chicken dish served widely across Singapore's Chinese-Singaporean restaurant and hawker scene, drawing culinary inspiration from the bold, numbing-spicy flavour profile of Sichuan (Szechuan) cuisine in China. The dish features boneless chicken pieces — typically breast or thigh — marinated and stir-fried or pan-fried at high heat, then tossed in a pungent Schezwan sauce made from dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and a small amount of sugar, producing a characteristic fiery, aromatic, and slightly tangy coating. Bell peppers and onions are commonly added for texture and colour. The dish is typically served as a main course alongside steamed white rice or flatbread. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 11.25 g protein, 8.75 g fat (saturated fat 2.0 g), and 10.0 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 300 mg — placing it in the moderate-sodium category. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low GI), making it suitable for moderate carbohydrate management. Clinically, the dish is of moderate concern for hypertension due to sauce-derived sodium, and of moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to sauce sugars and starch thickeners, though the protein-fat matrix moderates glycaemic response. Cardiovascular risk is low-to-moderate given the low saturated fat content. Rheumatoid arthritis patients with nightshade sensitivity should exercise caution due to chilli and bell pepper content. The dish is halal-suitable and dairy-free. It is commonly known by spelling variants including Szechuan Chicken, Szechwan Chicken, and Sichuan Spicy Chicken.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Schezwan chicken?
Is Schezwan chicken suitable for diabetics?
Is Schezwan chicken high in sodium?
Can I eat Schezwan chicken with hypertension?
What is the difference between Schezwan and Sichuan chicken?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Spicy Schezwan Chicken exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central stir-fried chicken data combined with sauce component estimates. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable mixed stir-fried chicken dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried chicken dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific dish preparation is not reported in available approved sources; estimated potassium from chicken and vegetables (~200 mg/100 g) is noted in health context but not entered as a confirmed value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in approved sources at the required specificity level. 4. The input record described this dish as 'Turkish-inspired' with ethnic tags of 'Turkish', 'Kebab', and 'Shawarma' — these are factually incorrect. Schezwan/Sichuan chicken is a Chinese-origin dish adapted in Singapore; cultural metadata has been corrected accordingly. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.05 g/100 g — this is a trace value from cooking oil and is not naturally zero; it is a confirmed low value consistent with USDA data for stir-fried dishes using vegetable oil. 6. Nutrient source is an adapted composite estimate; a direct approved-source match does not exist for this dish. 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Spicy Schezwan Chicken was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. Ontology match types corrected to valid enumeration values: foodon_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (FOODON_03302757 chicken dish is the nearest broader class); foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent' (A00GJ cooked chicken meat is the nearest defensible parent anchor). 9. Aliases consistency self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language (english, chinese, malay, tamil, hindi, romanized) verified present verbatim in the flat aliases array. All 16 flat alias entries confirmed consistent with aliases_by_language values.
